  "title_inferred": "Madder Root (Qiancao) — Names, Description, and Medicinal Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】（音茜）、茅蒐（音搜）、茹藘（音如閭）、地血（《別錄》）、染緋草（《蜀本》）、血見愁（《土宿》）、風車草（《土宿》）、過山龍（《補遺》）、牛蔓。

《本草》言東方有而少，不如西方多，則西草為茜，又以此也。陸璣云：齊人謂之茜，徐人謂之牛蔓。又草之盛者為蒐，牽引為茹，連覆為藘，則所化之說，恐亦俗傳耳。《土宿真君本草》云：四補草，其根茜草也。一名西天王草，一名四藏草。

藏器曰：有名未用，苗根，即茜根也。茜、苗二字相似，傳寫之誤爾。宜並之。

【集解】《別錄》曰：茜根生喬山川谷。二月、三月採根曝乾。

又曰：苗根生山陰谷中。蔓草木上。莖有刺，實如椒。

弘景曰：此即今染絳茜草也。東間諸處乃有而少，不如西多。《詩》云茹藘在阪者是也。

保升曰：染緋草，葉似棗葉，頭尖下闊，莖葉俱澀，四、五葉對生節間，蔓延草木上。根紫赤色，所在皆有，八月採。

頌曰：今圃人亦作畦種蒔。故《史記》云：千畝梔、茜，其人與千戶侯等，言其利濃也。

時珍曰：茜草，十二月生苗，蔓延數尺。方莖中空有筋，外有細刺，數寸一節。每節五葉，葉如烏藥葉而糙澀，面青背綠。七、八月開花，結實如小椒大，中有細子。

根

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。權曰：甘。大明曰：酸。入藥炒用。震亨曰：熱。

元素曰：微酸、鹹，溫。陰中之陰。

《別錄》曰：苗根：鹹，平，無毒。

之才曰：畏鼠姑。汁，製雄黃。

【主治】寒濕風痺，黃疸，補中（《本經》）。止血，內崩下血，膀胱不足，跌蠱毒。久服益精氣，輕身。可以行血瀉血（甄權）。排膿。酒煎服（大明）。通經脈，治骨節風痛，活血行血（時珍）。

【發明】藏器曰：茜草主蠱毒，煮汁服。《周禮》：庶氏掌除蠱毒，以嘉草攻之。嘉草者，荷與茜也，主蠱為最。

震亨曰：俗人治痛風，用草藥取速效。如石絲為君，過山龍等佐之。皆性熱而燥，不能養陰，卻能燥濕病之淺者。濕痰得燥而開，瘀血得熱而行，故亦暫效。若病深而血少者，則愈劫愈虛而病愈深矣。

時珍曰：茜根赤色而氣溫，味微酸而帶鹹。色赤入營，氣溫行滯，味酸入肝而鹹走血，手足厥陰血分之藥也，專於行血活血。俗方用治女子經水不通，以一兩煎酒服之，一日即通，甚效。《名醫別錄》言其久服益精氣輕身，《日華子》言其泄精，殊不相合，恐未可憑。",
  "translation": "**[Names]** Qian [音茜; 'madder'], Maosou [茅蒐, 音搜], Rülü [茹藘, 音如閭], Dixue ['earth blood'] (Bielu), Ranfei Cao ['crimson-dyeing herb'] (Shuben), Xuejian Chou ['blood-distressing herb'] (Tusu), Fengche Cao ['windmill herb'] (Tusu), Guoshan Long ['mountain-crossing dragon'] (Buyi), and Niuman ['ox vine'].

The Bencao states that [this plant] exists in the east but is scarce there, being far more abundant in the west; hence the 'western herb' (xi cao) gives rise to the name qian [茜], and this is another reason for it. Lu Ji states: the people of Qi call it qian; the people of Xu call it niuman. Furthermore, 'grass in abundance' is sou [蒐]; 'trailing and pulling' is rü [茹]; 'intertwining and covering' is lü [藘] — yet these etymological derivations are likely no more than popular tradition. The Tusu Zhenjun Bencao states: 'Four-supplement herb' (sìbǔ cǎo) — its root is qiancao [madder root]. It is

results.title_inferred: Jiancao (Clipping Herb) — Roots: Uses and Preparations
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰：剪草生山澤間，葉如茗而細，江東用之。

頌曰：生潤州。二月、三月採，曝乾用。

時珍曰：按：許叔微《本事方》言：剪草狀如茜草，又如細辛。婺、台二州皆有之，惟婺州者可用。其說殊詳，今遍詢訪無識者。或云即茜草也，未有的據。

根

【氣味】苦，涼，無毒。頌曰：平。

【主治】諸惡瘡、疥癬、風瘙，蝕有蟲，浸酒服（大明）。

【發明】元素曰：上部血，須用剪草、牡丹皮、天門冬、麥門冬。

時珍曰：許學士《本事方》云：剪草治勞瘵吐血損肺及血妄行，名曰神傳膏。其法：每用一斤淨洗，晒爲末，入生蜜二斤，和爲膏，以器盛之，不得犯鐵器，一日一蒸，九蒸九曝乃止。病患五更起，面東坐，不得語言，以匙抄藥四匙食之，良久以稀粟米飲壓之。藥只冷服，米飲亦勿大熱，或吐或下不妨。如久病肺損咯血，只一服愈。尋常嗽血妄行，每服一匙可也。有一貴婦病瘵，得此方，九日藥成。前一夕，病者夢人戒令翌日勿亂服藥。次日將服藥，屋上土墜器中，不可用。再合成，將服，爲貓覆器，又不得食。再合未就，而夫人卒矣。此藥之異有如此。若小小血妄行，只一啜而愈也。此藥絕妙若此，而世失傳，惜哉！
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations**

Zangqi [Chen Zangqi] states: Jiancao [Clipping Herb] grows in mountain marshes and wetlands. Its leaves resemble those of the tea plant [Camellia sinensis] but are finer. It is used in the Jiangdong region.

Su Song states: It grows in Runzhou [modern Zhenjiang, Jiangsu]. It is gathered in the second and third months [of the lunar calendar] and dried in the sun for use.

Li Shizhen states: According to Xu Shuwei's [Xu Shuwei, styled Xueshi, Song dynasty physician] *Benshifang* [Tried and Tested Prescriptions], jiancao resembles madder [Rubia cordifolia, i.e., qiancao] in appearance, and also resembles wild ginger [Asarum, i.e., xixin]. It is found in both Wuzhou and Taizhou, but only the variety from Wuzhou is suitable for use. This account is quite detailed, yet after extensive inquiry I have found no one today who recognizes it. Some say it is simply madder [qiancao], but there is no definitive evidence for this.

---

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, cool, non-toxic. Su Song states: neutral [ping].

**Indications [Zhuzhi]:** All manner of malignant sores [e'chuang], scabies and tinea [jiexuan], wind-induced itching [fengsao], and ulcerative lesions infested with parasites. Prepared by steeping in wine and taken orally. (Da Ming [Rihuazi Bencao])

**Elaboration [Faming]:**

Zhang Yuansu [Zhang Yuansu, Jin dynasty physician] states: For bleeding disorders of the upper body, one must use jiancao, moutan bark [Paeonia suffruticosa, mudanpi], asparagus tuber [Asparagus cochinchinensis, tianmendong], and ophiopogon [Ophiopogon japonicus, maimendong].

Li Shizhen states: The Scholar Xu's [Xu Shuwei's] *Benshifang* records: Jiancao treats consumptive disease [laozhai] with hemoptysis, lung damage, and uncontrolled bleeding [xue wang xing]. The preparation is called *Shenchuangao* [Divinely Transmitted Paste]. The method: take one jin [catty], wash clean, dry in the sun, and grind to a powder. Add two jin of raw honey [sheng mi], blend into a paste, and store in a vessel — iron vessels must not be used. Steam it once daily for nine steamings and nine sun-dryings, then stop. The patient rises at the fifth watch [before dawn], sits facing east, and must not speak. Using a spoon, eat four spoonfuls of the medicine, and after a good while follow it down with thin millet gruel [xi sumiyin]. The medicine is to be taken only cold; the millet gruel should also not be too hot. Whether vomiting or purging results, this is no cause for alarm. For chronic lung damage with blood-spitting, a single course suffices for recovery. For ordinary coughing of blood and uncontrolled bleeding, one spoonful per dose is enough.

There was a noblewoman who suffered from consumption. She obtained this prescription and after nine days the medicine was ready. On the eve of taking it, the patient dreamed that someone warned her not to take the medicine carelessly the next day. The following day, just as she was about to take the medicine, soil fell from the roof into the vessel, rendering it unusable. The medicine was compounded again and she was about to take it when a cat knocked over the vessel, so again she could not consume it. The medicine was being prepared a third time when the lady suddenly died.

Such is the strange nature of this medicine. For minor cases of uncontrolled bleeding, a single sip suffices for a cure. That a medicine so supremely efficacious as this has been lost to the world — how lamentable!

entities_mentioned.name: 剪草 (Jiancao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Identity uncertain; possibly Rubia cordifolia (madder/qiancao) or an Asarum species; Li Shizhen himself could not identify it definitively
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen notes that despite extensive inquiry no one in his time recognized the plant, and the suggestion that it equals madder (qiancao) lacked firm evidence.

entities_mentioned.name: 茜草 (Qiancao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubia cordifolia (Indian madder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a plant jiancao supposedly resembles in appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xixin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum species (wild ginger), likely Asarum sieboldii or Asarum heterotropoides
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as another plant jiancao resembles in appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡丹皮 (Mudanpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa (tree peony) root bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Zhang Yuansu as part of a formula for upper-body bleeding disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 天門冬 (Tianmendong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus cochinchinensis (Chinese asparagus tuber)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Zhang Yuansu alongside jiancao for upper-body bleeding.

entities_mentioned.name: 麥門冬 (Maimendong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiopogon japonicus (lilyturf / mondo grass tuber)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Zhang Yuansu alongside jiancao for upper-body bleeding.

entities_mentioned.name: 神傳膏 (Shenchuangao)
entities_mentioned.notes: A named paste preparation of jiancao with raw honey, used for consumptive disease, hemoptysis, and uncontrolled bleeding; described in Xu Shuwei's Benshifang.

results.translator_notes: 1. The anecdote about the noblewoman (貴婦) who failed three times to take the medicine and then died is presented by Li Shizhen as evidence of the drug's mysterious or numinous quality (藥之異), consistent with the broader medieval Chinese view that efficacious drugs could be supernaturally 'protected.' 2. The instruction to sit facing east and remain silent during administration reflects common ritual precautions in Song–Ming-era medical prescriptions. 3. '五更' (fifth watch) refers to approximately 3–5 a.m. 4. 'Iron vessels must not be used' (不得犯鐵器) is a recurring pharmaceutical caution in Chinese herbalism, reflecting beliefs about metallic contamination of honey-based preparations. 5. The name '許叔微' is the given name; his courtesy title (字) was Zhike and his honorific was Xueshi (學士), hence 'Scholar Xu' in later references within the same passage. 6. OCR issue corrected: '蝕有蟲' was rendered clearly enough to retain as-is; it refers to ulcerative lesions with worm/parasite infestation.

  "original_text": "【釋名】木通（士良）、附支（《本經》）、時珍曰︰有細細孔，兩頭皆通，故名通草，即今所謂木通也。今之通草，乃古之通脫木也。宋本草混注爲一，名實相亂，今分出之。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰通草，生石城山谷及山陽。正月、二月採枝，陰乾。

弘景曰︰今出近道。繞樹藤生，汁白。莖有細孔，兩頭皆通。含一頭吹之，則氣出彼頭者良。或云即藤莖也。

恭曰︰此物大者徑三寸，每節有二、三枝，枝頭有五葉。子長三、四寸，核黑瓤白，食之甘美。南人謂爲燕覆子，或名烏覆子。遇七、八月採之。

藏器曰︰江東人呼爲蓄子，江西人呼爲拿子，如袋，瓤黃子黑，食之去皮。蘇云色白者，乃猴也。

頌曰︰今澤、潞、漢中、江淮、湖南州郡亦有之。藤生，蔓大如指，其莖幹大者徑三寸。一枝五葉，頗類石葦，又似芍藥，三葉相對。夏秋開紫花，亦有白花者。結實如小木瓜，食之甘美，即陳士良本草所謂桴子也。其枝，今人古方所用通草，皆今之木通，其通脫木稀有用者。或以木通爲葡萄苗者，非矣。按︰張氏《燕吳行紀》載︰揚州甘泉東院兩廊前有通草，其形如椿不同，或別一物也。

時珍曰︰今之木通，有紫、白二色︰紫者皮濃味辛；白者皮薄味淡。《本經》言味辛，《別錄》言味甘，是二者皆能通利也。

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。《別錄》曰︰甘。權曰︰微寒。

普曰︰神農、黃帝︰辛；雷公︰苦。

杲曰︰味甘而淡，氣平味薄。降也，陽中陰也。甘，寒，無毒。

詵曰︰平，南人多食之，北人不知其功。

【主治】除脾胃寒熱，通利九竅血脈關節，令人不忘，去惡蟲（《本經》）。療脾疸，常欲眠，心煩噦，出音聲，治耳聾，散癰腫諸結不消，及金瘡惡瘡，鼠折，鼻息肉，墮胎，去三蟲（《別錄》）。治五淋，利小便，開關格，治人多睡，主水腫浮大（甄權）。利諸經脈寒熱不通之氣（孟詵）。理風熱，小便數急疼，小腹虛滿，宜煎湯並蔥食之，有效（士良）。安心除煩，止渴退熱，明耳目，治鼻塞，通小腸，下水，破積聚血塊，排膿，治瘡癤，止痛，催生下胞，女人血閉，月候不勻，天行時疾，頭痛目眩，羸劣乳結，及下乳（大明）。利大小便，令人心寬，下氣（藏器）。主諸瘡，喉痺咽痛，濃煎含咽（珣）。通經利竅，導小腸火（杲）。項下癭瘤（甄權）。

子
濃腸胃，令人能食，下三焦惡氣，續五臟斷絕氣，使語聲足氣，通十二經脈。和核食之（孟詵）。除三焦客熱，胃口熱閉，反胃不下食（士良）。止渴，利小便（時珍）。

【發明】杲曰︰本草十劑︰通可去滯，通草、防己之屬是也。夫防己大苦寒，能瀉血中濕熱之滯，又通大便。通草甘淡，能助西方秋氣下降，利小便，專瀉氣滯也。肺受熱邪，津液氣化之原絕，則寒水斷流，膀胱受濕熱，癃閉約縮，小便不通，宜此治之。其症胸中煩熱，口燥舌乾，咽乾，大渴引飲，小便淋瀝，或閉塞不通，脛酸腳熱，並宜通草主之。凡氣味與之同者，茯苓、澤瀉、燈草、豬苓、琥珀、瞿麥、車前子之類，皆可以滲濕利小便，泄其滯氣也。又曰︰木通下行，泄小腸火，利小便，與琥珀同功，無他藥可比。

時珍曰︰木通手厥陰心包絡、手足太陽小腸、膀胱之藥也。故上能通心清肺，治頭痛，利九竅；下能泄濕熱，利小便，通大腸，治遍身拘痛。《本經》及《別錄》皆不言及利小便治淋之功，甄權、日華子輩始發揚之。蓋其能泄丙丁之火，則肺不受邪，能通水道。水源既清，則津液自化，而諸經之濕與熱，皆由小便泄去。故古方導赤散用之，亦瀉南補北、扶西抑東之意。楊仁齋《直指方》",
  "translation": "**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also called Mù Tōng [木通] (by Chén Shìliáng), Fùzhī [附支] (in the *Shennong Bencao Jing*). Li Shizhen states: It has fine continuous pores running through from one end to the other, hence the name Tōng Cǎo ["through-herb"]. What is called Tōng Cǎo today is in fact the ancient Tōng Tuō Mù [通脫木]. The Song-dynasty materia medica conflated the two into one entry, creating confusion between name and substance; I have here separated them.
